title: Native American societies before European contact
description: Practice your historical skills with a quiz on pre-contact native societies.
topic_path: Native American societies before European contact

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below.

>"Around 7,000 years ago, agriculture emerged in Mesoamerica,
including the domestication of maize, beans, and squash, causing major changes in the plants that people cultivated. Three sisters agriculture had spread across Mexico by 3,500 years ago, though they originated at different times."

>-Source: Amanda J. Landon, anthropologist, "The 'How' of the Three Sisters," 2008

**Why was this method of planting important?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: These three crops mutually supported each other's growth, a system that spread from present-day Mexico northward into the present-day American Southwest.
These three crops attracted wild turkeys and other wild game, which made hunting easier for more sedentary communities.
These three were “cash crops” for societies like the Iroquois and Susquehannocks, as these were the only commodity they could sell to the English to fund their Confederacy
These three crops were arranged ceremonially so that they could be used for religious purposes as offerings for the gods

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below.

>"Around 7,000 years ago, agriculture emerged in Mesoamerica,
including the domestication of maize, beans, and squash, causing major changes in the plants that people cultivated. Three sisters agriculture had spread across Mexico by 3,500 years ago, though they originated at different times."

>-Source: Amanda J. Landon, anthropologist, "The 'How' of the Three Sisters," 2008

**How did the cultivation of maize affect settlement patterns in the American Southwest and present-day Mexico?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It caused Native Americans in the area to develop permanent settlements supported by farming and irrigation systems.
It caused Native Americans in the area to live a hunter-gatherer lifestyle, where most of their diet was dependent on animals they hunted.
It caused Native Americans in the area to live a nomadic lifestyle, constantly searching for new ground to cultivate.
It caused Native Americans in the area to develop permanent settlements supported by a hunter-gatherer lifestyle.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below.

>"Around 7,000 years ago, agriculture emerged in Mesoamerica,
including the domestication of maize, beans, and squash, causing major changes in the plants that people cultivated. Three sisters agriculture had spread across Mexico by 3,500 years ago, though they originated at different times."

>-Source: Amanda J. Landon, anthropologist, "The 'How' of the Three Sisters," 2008

**Which of the following pieces of evidence would best support Landon’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: archaeological remains of ancient seeds
written inventories of annual harvests
oral histories of hunting and gathering
fossilized animal bones and teeth

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below.

![Painting depicting Sioux teepees.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/604e0414be8df214fd12483c80eea042.jpg)
*A depiction of Sioux teepees, painted by Karl Bodmer in 1833.* Image credit: [WikiArt](https://www.wikiart.org/en/karl-bodmer/sioux-teepee-from-volume-1-of-travels-in-the-interior-of-north-america-1833)

**Which of the following factors most influenced the adoption of largely mobile lifestyles by early societies in the Great Basin and Great Plains?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The arid Great Basin limited farming opportunities close to major waterways, making permanent settlements undesirable.
Religious beliefs stipulated that devout individuals should live nomadic lives.
Frequent earthquakes and flash floods made stone houses and pueblos unsustainable.
The climate of the Great Plains consisted of periods of drought, making farming impossible.

assessment_items.question_content:
![Teepee](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ca067f9722b342f1c70453c664bbd282.jpg)
*A depiction of Sioux teepees, painted by Karl Bodmer in 1833.* Image credit: [WikiArt](https://www.wikiart.org/en/karl-bodmer/sioux-teepee-from-volume-1-of-travels-in-the-interior-of-north-america-1833)

**Why did the Sioux live in structures like those depicted in the image?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They facilitated a nomadic lifestyle
They were easily disposable
They reinforced egalitarian social structures
They were more durable than other forms of housing

assessment_items.question_content:
![Watercolor painting of teepees](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ca067f9722b342f1c70453c664bbd282.jpg)
*A depiction of Sioux teepees, painted by Karl Bodmer in 1833.* Image credit: [WikiArt](https://www.wikiart.org/en/karl-bodmer/sioux-teepee-from-volume-1-of-travels-in-the-interior-of-north-america-1833)

**Native Americans lived in teepees as they followed herds of bison across the Great Plains. Which of the following environmental factors led bison to flourish in the Great Plains?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The wet grasslands of the western Great Plains created prairie ideal for grazing.
The arrival of horses in 1519 made it easier for Native Americans to herd and breed bison.
The melting of glaciers provided new water sources for bison herds.
Bison were chased onto the Great Plains by prehistoric predators.

assessment_items.question_content:
![Aerial reconstruction of the city of Cahokia](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e4a5a861c4433aa8a4b8649ac757c926.jpg)
*Artist's conception of the city of Cahokia, near modern-day St. Louis. At its peak around 1100 CE, Cahokia had 20,000 residents.* Image credit: [Wikimedia Commons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cahokia#/media/File:Cahokia_Aerial_HRoe_2015.jpg)

**Which of the following conclusion might historians make about the Mississippian people from the evidence of Cahokia?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They lived in settled villages and farmed, allowing for the growth of large populations.
They lived a nomadic lifestyle, following herds of animals across the Plains.
They were not sophisticated enough to build lasting structures.
They preferred fishing for their food over farming.

assessment_items.question_content:
![Aerial reconstruction of the city of Cahokia](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e4a5a861c4433aa8a4b8649ac757c926.jpg)
*Artist's conception of the city of Cahokia, near modern-day St. Louis. At its peak around 1100 CE, Cahokia had 20,000 residents.* Image credit: [Wikimedia Commons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cahokia#/media/File:Cahokia_Aerial_HRoe_2015.jpg)

**How were Mississippian people similar to other Native American groups in the pre-contact era?**

 
[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The environment in which they lived influenced their societies and economies.
They were largely nomadic, depending on horses to follow herds of animals.
They had no religious practices or beliefs.
They had an egalitarian social structure, where individuals were generally equal in status.

assessment_items.question_content:
![Aerial reconstruction of the city of Cahokia](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e4a5a861c4433aa8a4b8649ac757c926.jpg)
*Artist's conception of the city of Cahokia, near modern-day St. Louis. At its peak around 1100 CE, Cahokia had 20,000 residents.* Image credit: [Wikimedia Commons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cahokia#/media/File:Cahokia_Aerial_HRoe_2015.jpg)

**Based on the evidence of Cahokia, which of the following might historians conclude about the Mississippian peoples? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They had significant social organization, which allowed them to coordinate the construction of large buildings.
They rarely went to war with other tribes, which gave them time to focus on art and music.
They were deeply religious, worshiping a diverse pantheon of gods.
They depended on fishing for their main source of food.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

> “There were four chiefs: Mr. Bear, Cougar, Bald Eagle, and Salmon. They met to try to figure out what it was that they were going to do. They knew of a place where there were many salmon. This would be the best thing of the coming people. It would keep them strong and healthy; besides, it would taste so good! But there was a problem. The salmon were being held way up river by a dam and were being guarded by some women, who when crossed would stop at nothing to destroy anything that got in the way of keeping their salmon and doing their work.”

-Native American legend recorded by Martin Louie and Diana Brooks. Published in *Wicazo Sa Review*, 1990. 

**This passage provides evidence to support which of the following assertions about Native American societies more broadly?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Native Americans’ food sources and environment shaped their living patterns.
Native Americans relied on three-sister farming.
Native American societies only hunted salmon, and no other fish.
Native American women fished, while Native American men tended crops.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

> “There were four chiefs: Mr. Bear, Cougar, Bald Eagle, and Salmon. They met to try to figure out what it was that they were going to do. They knew of a place where there were many salmon. This would be the best thing of the coming people. It would keep them strong and healthy; besides, it would taste so good! But there was a problem. The salmon were being held way up river by a dam and were being guarded by some women, who when crossed would stop at nothing to destroy anything that got in the way of keeping their salmon and doing their work.”

-Native American legend recorded by Martin Louie and Diana Brooks. Published in *Wicazo Sa Review*, 1990. 

**Based on passage, in which region of North America do you think the Native American people who told this legend lived?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the Pacific Northwest
the Great Plains
the Southwest
the Northeast

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

> “There were four chiefs: Mr. Bear, Cougar, Bald Eagle, and Salmon. They met to try to figure out what it was that they were going to do. They knew of a place where there were many salmon. This would be the best thing of the coming people. It would keep them strong and healthy; besides, it would taste so good! But there was a problem. The salmon were being held way up river by a dam and were being guarded by some women, who when crossed would stop at nothing to destroy anything that got in the way of keeping their salmon and doing their work.”

-Native American legend recorded by Martin Louie and Diana Brooks. Published in *Wicazo Sa Review*, 1990. 

 **Which of the following was a major contrast between the Native Americans described in the passage and those living in the Great Plains region?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Native Americans in the Northwest relied on fishing, while Native Americans on the Plains relied on hunting to obtain food.
Native Americans in the Pueblos relied on irrigation to water their crops, while Native Americans on the Plains relied on rain to water their crops.
Native Americans in the Southeast built mounds to bury their dead, while Native Americans on the Plains cremated their dead in funeral pyres.
Native Americans in the Northeast lived in settled villages, while Native Americans on the Plains lived a highly mobile lifestyle.
